narrative_blocks.header: Acquitted because her beer was too strong
narrative_blocks.body: In 1653 at the family tavern in Newbury, she was 'presented' for selling beer above the legal price of two pennies per quart. She was acquitted when the court found her beer was much stronger than the ordinary.

narrative_blocks.header: Acquitted because her beer was too strong
narrative_blocks.body: In 1653 at the family tavern in Newbury, she was 'presented' for selling beer above the legal price of two pennies per quart. She was acquitted when the court found her beer was much stronger than the ordinary.

notable.notable_blurb: Nantucket founder and first chief magistrate

narrative_blocks.header: Bought Nantucket in 1659 with two beaver hats
narrative_blocks.body: On July 2, 1659 he led a group of Salisbury investors that bought Nantucket Island from Thomas Mayhew for thirty pounds and two beaver hats. He was given first choice of land and settled near Capaum pond on the island's west end.

narrative_blocks.header: Chief magistrate of Nantucket twice appointed
narrative_blocks.body: Appointed chief magistrate of Nantucket on June 29, 1671 — the most wealthy and respected of the colonists. He was again appointed chief magistrate in 1677 for a four-year term.

narrative_blocks.header: Most notable Nantucket Americans descend from him
narrative_blocks.body: His descendants ramified across colonial America — some became wealthy through whaling and the triangle trade, some Loyalists who fled to Canada, others Quaker abolitionists. Almost all notable Americans with Nantucket roots descend from him.

narrative_blocks.header: Sons Peter, Tristram Jr., and James settled Nantucket
narrative_blocks.body: His sons Peter Coffin, Tristram Coffin Jr., and James Coffin received land on Nantucket alongside him. James Coffin Esq. (1639-1720) is the ancestor of the Hooker-tree branch through his great-grandson James Coffin who married Priscilla Rawson.
